title: VPN Tunnel Keepalive Failure Analysis
spl:
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:system") earliest=-24h
| search "IKE" OR "keepalive" OR "DPD" OR "dead peer"
| stats count by tunnel_id, peer_ip, message_signature
| sort 40 -count
description: Tracks DPD/keepalive failures and tunnel teardown reasons for site-to-site and remote-access — isolates path MTU, NAT, and idle timeout issues.
value: Network operations teams monitor IKE/IPSec tunnel health through keepalive and Dead Peer Detection failures, identifying unstable WAN paths before tunnels drop and cause site-to-site connectivity outages.
implementation: Normalize `message_signature` with `rex` or `cluster` on raw. Correlate with UC-17.2.5 stability metrics.
visualization: Bar chart (failures by peer), Table (top messages), Line chart (failure rate).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (`sourcetype=cisco:asa`) or Palo Alto Networks Add-on (`sourcetype=pan:system`). Data in `index=vpn`.
• Key fields: `tunnel_id`, `peer_ip`, `action/event` (keepalive_failed, dpd_timeout, ike_timeout).
• IKE/IPSec tunnels use Dead Peer Detection (DPD) or keepalive messages to verify the remote peer is reachable. Failures indicate network path issues, peer reboots, or configuration drift.
• This UC focuses on site-to-site and remote-access IPSec tunnels, not SSL/TLS VPN sessions.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify keepalive/DPD events:
```spl
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:system") earliest=-24h
| search "keepalive" OR "DPD" OR "dead peer" OR "IKE.*timeout"
| stats count by host, peer_ip
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Keepalive failure analysis:
```spl
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:system") earliest=-24h
| search "keepalive" OR "DPD" OR "dead peer" OR "IKE.*timeout"
| stats count as failures earliest(_time) as first latest(_time) as last by host, peer_ip
| eval duration_hours=round((last - first)/3600, 1)
| eval frequency=if(duration_hours>0, round(failures/duration_hours, 1), failures)
| sort - frequency
```

Understanding this SPL: Tracks keepalive failures per tunnel peer. High frequency (many failures per hour) indicates an unstable path. Burst failures (many in a short period, then none) suggest a temporary network outage. Persistent low-rate failures suggest MTU issues or marginal link quality.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) On ASA: `show crypto isakmp sa` to check IKE SA status. Peers with DPD failures show as 'QM_IDLE' or missing.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Table of tunnel peers with failure counts. Timechart of failures.
Alert: Trigger when any peer exceeds 10 failures in 1h.

Runbook:
1. **Sustained failures** — Check the network path: traceroute, link utilization, ISP status.
2. **Burst then recovery** — Transient issue. Document and monitor.
3. **DPD timeout but tunnel still up** — DPD interval may be too aggressive. Increase from 10s to 30s.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No keepalive events** — DPD may not be enabled. On ASA: `crypto isakmp keepalive 30 5`.
• **Peer IP not matching expected** — NAT-T may be changing the peer address.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the heartbeat signals between our network tunnels, and when they start missing, we know the connection path is having trouble before it completely breaks.
